posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Does it even matter anymore for those who farm and select their grapes. Even “lesser” vintages it seems many produce great wines.

I haven’t closely followed the recent 2018+ seasons lately, but are their any of note?

I was browsing back at vintage charts and found WS note interesting from 2012.

“A challenging season, with a miraculous recovery at harvest yielding a small but exceptional crop; ripe and harmonious”

They did give it a 95, but I didn’t know it was in peril.

They also did give 2015 94-97….I did not realize it might be THAT well regarded either.
I have really enjoyed the 15’s from some growers but now I shudder to think what the big house’s may ultimately think of it.

Any thoughts…

posts.name: Suzanne Bayard
posts.cooked: I find 2013 to be a surprising hit that was a bit underrated at the time (Galloni at least seems to have come around on the vintage, particularly in the Cote des Blancs). The 2015 is mostly a miss for me. I get a lot of unpleasant green notes (pyrazines?) from almost every vintage and non-vintage 15s I’ve opened, but it’s my understanding that not everyone can taste that particular note. 2016s seem charming, if lacking in depth. 2017 for me is too ripe - the wines tend to lean heavy on fruit. I’ve not had a 2012 I’ve disliked, but I haven’t opened enough to have a solid opinion. Just my two cents.

Vintages still matter…and the differences in the vintages are what make the wines enjoyable. We did a 2011-2013 vertical tasting of the Pierre Peters Chettilons last June in 2021. There was a stark difference between each vintage. The '12 seemed to be the most complex & complete, the '13 was the most lean & precise, and the '11 was enjoyable, but lacked the clarity & complexity of the other two vintages. By itself, the 2011 was enjoyable, but put into context when tasting it side by side with the other vintages…it was not as complex. It reminded me of tasting a 1er cru beside a pair of Grand Crus.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked:
Kirk, I agree it does of course matter. My thought was that there are so many good grower producers that possibly can overcome the vintage charts in general and produce worthy wines in their own right. I find much respect for vintage variations though, especially in these producers that continue to put out non-declared wines.

That said it’s also very interesting to know what the houses may be thinking as their scope is much broader I would imagine.

That said I would like to hear what was good, bad, easy or challenging about certain years.

Vintages still matter…and the differences in the vintages are what make the wines enjoyable. We did a 2011-2013 vertical tasting of the Pierre Peters Chettilons last June in 2021. There was a stark difference between each vintage. The '12 seemed to be the most complex & complete, the '13 was the most lean & precise, and the '11 was enjoyable, but lacked the clarity & complexity of the other two vintages. By itself, the 2011 was enjoyable, but put into context when tasting it side by side with the other vintages…it was not as complex. It reminded me of tasting a 1er cru beside a pair of Grand Crus.

And Chétillons is arguably the Wine of the Vintage in 2011, which was generally weak. Agree with your general impressions. 2013 is the last classical vintage when people picked in October and the wines are tight and need time to develop. Despite the significant rain early in 2012 that was the cause of concern at the time, the latter part of the growing season was sunny and it drinks like a “solar” vintage.

While we won’t know for sure for years, the winemakers are talking about 12-13-14 and 18-19-20 as strong vintages, and a great deal of enthusiasm around 19 in particular.

The OP question is interesting, but perhaps a better one is “are most vintage Champagnes worth the premium to MV blends?”

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: If the past has revealed anything, it’s that down the road every heralded vintage will be revised to show its weakness. The Pinot Noir of 1996 and the Chadonnay of 2002 come to mind.

Agree with your general impressions. 2013 is the last classical vintage when people picked in October and the wines are tight and need time to develop. Despite the significant rain early in 2012 that was the cause of concern at the time, the latter part of the growing season was sunny and it drinks like a “solar” vintage.

While we won’t know for sure for years, the winemakers are talking about 12-13-14 and 18-19-20 as strong vintages, and a great deal of enthusiasm around 19 in particular.

The OP question is interesting, but perhaps a better one is “are most vintage Champagnes worth the premium to MV blends?”

2011 Chétillons and Vilmart Coeur de Cuvée share the WOTV honor.

posts.name: John Telford
posts.cooked: The simple answer is yes vintages matter in Champagne. However/But less than in other areas because of the ability to blend----both past vintages and “in vintage” blending. However…with the dawn of “grower” champagnes it matters more than “established houses”.

+1 on the Vilmart. I found the Chetillons lacking in its usual complexity and minerality, but I tasted on release.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Vintages still matter…and the differences in the vintages are what make the wines enjoyable. We did a 2011-2013 vertical tasting of the Pierre Peters Chettilons last June in 2021. There was a stark difference between each vintage. The '12 seemed to be the most complex & complete, the '13 was the most lean & precise, and the '11 was enjoyable, but lacked the clarity & complexity of the other two vintages. By itself, the 2011 was enjoyable, but put into context when tasting it side by side with the other vintages…it was not as complex. It reminded me of tasting a 1er cru beside a pair of Grand Crus.

And Chétillons is arguably the Wine of the Vintage in 2011, which was generally weak. Agree with your general impressions. 2013 is the last classical vintage when people picked in October and the wines are tight and need time to develop. Despite the significant rain early in 2012 that was the cause of concern at the time, the latter part of the growing season was sunny and it drinks like a “solar” vintage.

While we won’t know for sure for years, the winemakers are talking about 12-13-14 and 18-19-20 as strong vintages, and a great deal of enthusiasm around 19 in particular.

2011 Chétillons and Vilmart Coeur de Cuvée share the WOTV honor.

I would put Vilmart’s Blanches Voies 2011 ahead of their CdC, and Larmandier-Bernier’s VVdL and Chemins d’Avize a touch ahead of both, at least for now!

Yeah. I’ve had these side by side and frankly it’s not that close for me. Vilmart all the way.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: To get into the more general question, clearly modern agronomy combined with the economic viability of extreme selection where necessary have done much to mitigate adverse vintage variation. The ideal that an estate such as e.g. Château Lafleur or Domaine Leroy aspires to is variation in style but not in quality. Between producers who have the capacity and willingness to work this way—and the support of the market to do it (because it’s very expensive)—and the rest, there is a gap that opens up in very challenging vintages. This makes generalizing about a vintage a bit like advising someone who is about to cross a river that it is three feet deep on average. Your mileage may vary!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Move to France, you can buy it from their website.

posts.name: Andy Ma
posts.cooked:
I find 2013 to be a surprising hit that was a bit underrated at the time (Galloni at least seems to have come around on the vintage, particularly in the Cote des Blancs). The 2015 is mostly a miss for me. I get a lot of unpleasant green notes (pyrazines?) from almost every vintage and non-vintage 15s I’ve opened, but it’s my understanding that not everyone can taste that particular note. 2016s seem charming, if lacking in depth. 2017 for me is too ripe - the wines tend to lean heavy on fruit. I’ve not had a 2012 I’ve disliked, but I haven’t opened enough to have a solid opinion. Just my two cents.

I’ve been curious about the 2015 “grassiness” since Terry Thiese started writing about it a few years ago. He posted some new notes recently and still gets it. The 2015s I’ve had are the Agrapart Mineral and Laherte Freres LGC and I didn’t get it at all. Also Collin Maillons and Roises, Bouchard Le Haut Lemblee, Larmandier Bernier LON15, nothing there either. My sample size is mostly Chardonnay though. Which wines did you taste where it was noticeable?

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
I was browsing back at vintage charts and found WS note interesting from 2012.

“A challenging season, with a miraculous recovery at harvest yielding a small but exceptional crop; ripe and harmonious”

They did give it a 95, but I didn’t know it was in peril.

There is a documentary that should be widely available to stream, “A Year in Champagne,” that follows a few makers during the 2012 season. It’s on Amazon Prime and a few other services.

posts.name: Suzanne Bayard
posts.cooked:
I find 2013 to be a surprising hit that was a bit underrated at the time (Galloni at least seems to have come around on the vintage, particularly in the Cote des Blancs). I get a lot of unpleasant green notes (pyrazines?) from almost every vintage and non-vintage 15s I’ve opened, but it’s my understanding that not everyone can taste that particular note. 2016s seem charming, if lacking in depth. 2017 for me is too ripe - the wines tend to lean heavy on fruit. I’ve not had a 2012 I’ve disliked, but I haven’t opened enough to have a solid opinion. Just my two cents.

I’ve been curious about the 2015 “grassiness” since Terry Thiese started writing about it a few years ago. He posted some new notes recently and still gets it. The 2015s I’ve had are the Agrapart Mineral and Laherte Freres LGC and I didn’t get it at all. Also Collin Maillons and Roises, Bouchard Le Haut Lemblee, Larmandier Bernier LON15, nothing there either. My sample size is mostly Chardonnay though. Which wines did you taste where it was noticeable?

I first noticed it in the Pierre Peters NV 2015-base. I’ve tasted it in the 2015-base Pierre Peters Rose for Albane, Marc Hebrart 2015s, Pierre Gerbais, Gaston Chicquet, Vouette e Sorbee, among others. It’s my understanding that not everyone can taste it.
